title: Red clay cold-process soap
subtitle: A terracotta-coloured bar with red Moroccan clay, olive oil, and sweet almond oil
excerpt: Red Moroccan clay (also sold as red rhassoul) gives this bar a warm terracotta tone and a fine-grain texture in the lather. The formula uses olive oil and sweet almond oil as the main conditioning fats, with coconut oil for lather. A clean, unscented bar suitable for normal to oily skin.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, sweet almond oil 0.136. 5% superfat applied. Red Moroccan clay in soapmaking: Bramble Berry colourant and additive reference (2023). Rhassoul clay mineral composition and cosmetic use: Moroccan Institute of Research and Studies (IMRA) technical data, referenced in International Journal of Cosmetic Science (2009).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by running revised oil weights through a lye calculator. Clay amount scales at 1 tablespoon per 250 g oil.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-process soap requires 48 hours in the mould before unmoulding and 4 to 6 weeks to cure fully.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark cupboard. Discard if the bar develops orange oily spots or a rancid smell. The red-brown colour is stable and does not fade.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter emulsifies to a thick custard. Clay is added at light trace and stirred in before the batter thickens further.
The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap. Each oil has a SAP value giving the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.

content.text: Red Moroccan clay is a mineral-rich clay with a fine particle size that disperses evenly through soap batter, giving a warm terracotta colour. In the lather, the clay adds a slight creamy texture. The oil base is olive oil and sweet almond oil for conditioning, with coconut oil at 25% for a firm bar and good lather. This is an unscented recipe that shows off the clay's colour without competition.

items.prepNote: 1 tablespoon; dispersed in a small amount of oil before adding at trace

content.text: Work in a ventilated space with safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves. Disperse the red clay in a tablespoon of olive oil taken from the weighed batch, stirring until the clay is fully absorbed and no lumps remain. Set aside.

Add the sodium hydroxide to the distilled water in a steady stream, stirring. Never add water to lye. The solution heats sharply and gives off fumes; do not lean over the pitcher. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.
Melt the coconut oil and combine with the olive and sweet almond oils. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C. Pour the lye solution into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend to light
. Stir in the clay paste and mix thoroughly. The terracotta colour deepens during the gel phase inside the mould.
Pour into the mould, cover, and wrap in a towel. Leave 48 hours before unmoulding. The
process continues during the cure. Cut into bars and cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks. The colour is fully stable after 2 weeks.
Store cured bars on an open soap dish. A cool dark cupboard is best for bars not in immediate use. Keeps two years. Discard if bars develop orange oily spots or a rancid smell.
